title: 第 19 節
en: KALPA. 183 were preserved. But, in spite of Wolf's maxim, that prose literature marks everywhere the introduction of writing, we must claim for the Brahmanas, as well as for the hymns, a certain period during which they were preserved by means of oral tradition only. Without the admission of an oral tradition, carried on for several generations and in several places by different families and Brahmanic colonies, it would seem impossible to account for the numerous recensions of the same Brahmana, and for the various readings of each recension. How the changes, the additions, the rearrangements of the original collections of the Brahmanas were effected, we have no means of ascertaining ; but we can see, that the Kanva and Madhyandina recensions of the Sathapata-brahmana presuppose some point from whence they both started in common. The same applies to the Bahvrichabrahmana in the widely differing recensions of the Aitareyins, the Sankhayanins or the Kaushitakins. There is a common stock in the Brahmanas of each Veda. The same ceremonial is described, the same doubts are raised, similar solutions are proposed, and many chapters are repeated in the same words. Before each recension took its present shape — and few only of these numerous recensions have been preserved to us — they must have rolled from hand to hand, sometimes losing old, sometimes gathering new matter ; now broken to pieces, now rearranged, till at last the name of their author became merged in the name of the Charana that preserved his work. No traces of this kind can be discovered in the Sutras. We probably read them in our MSS. exactly as they were written down at first by Katyayana, Asvalayana and others. They are evidently the works of indivi- K 4 184 KALPA.

dual writers, the result of careful and systematic research. They presuppose the Sanhitas and the Sakhas of the Sanhitas ; they presuppose the Brahmanas and the Sakhas of the Brahmanas. And they also refer to individual writers, whether they had become the founders of Charanas, or whether they enjoyed an authority as teachers of law and other subjects connected with the intellectual pursuits of the early Brahmans.

There is, however, one fact that seems to militate against the distinction between the Brahmanas and Sutras, in so far as it assigns a very early origin, and a traditionary character, to at least some works which were written in Sutras. At the time of Katyayana, if not at the time of Panini, there existed Sutras, which were not then considered as the works of modern or at least well-known authors, like Asvalayana or Katyayana, but indicated by their very name, that they had formed, for a time, part of the traditional literary property of a Charana, or of some learned school. Their titles are formed on the same principle as the titles of ancient Brahmanas. The affix in (nini) is added to the names of their reputed authors, and this, as we know, is a mark that their authors were considered as Rishis or inspired writers.1 Their works are not quoted in the singular, like all modern Sutras (for instance, " this is the ceremonial of Asmaratha," iti kalpa asmarathah), but, — and this is a characteristic feature of the ancient traditional literature of India — in the masculine plural, the literary works being supposed 1 Cf. Pan. iv. 3. 103—110. The Sutras from 106 are not explained in the Mahabhashya according to the Calcutta edition.

KALPA. 185 to have their only substantial existence in the minds or memories of those persons who read or taught them. We find, for instance, " thus say the Paralarins, the Sailalins, the Karmandins, the Krisasvins," whereas the work even of Panini himself is quoted as " the Paniniyam," as it were " Panineum," not as " the Panineyins." * But although these quotations refer to Sutras, it ought to be observed that not one of them refers to Kalpa, or ceremonial Sutras. Where P&nini (iv. 3. 105.), or rather his commentator, quotes works on Kalpa in a similar, though not in exactly the same manner, we must bear in mind that expressions like " Paingi kalpah," " the ceremonial taught by the old sage Pinga, " Kausiki kalpah," " the ceremonial taught by the old sage Kusika," may refer to portions of the Brahmanas which are called kalpa, ceremonial, in contradistinction to the brahmana or the purely theological discussions 2 ; and it is nowhere said that these old Kalpas were written in 1 Kalpa-sutras were composed contemporaneously with Panini, and even after his time, as, for instance, the Sutras of Asvalayana and Katyayana, which we still possess, and those of Asmarathya, which are lost. The last are quoted in the commentary to Panini (iv. 3. 105.), as a modern work on Kalpa ; yet Asvalayana in his Sutras, v. 13., refers to Asmarathya as an authority, whom he follows in opposition to other teachers whose opinion he rejects. Cf. Asv. Sutra, v. 13. ; Indische Studien, i. 45.

2 The wording of the Sutra, "Puranaprokteshu brahmanakalpeshu" seems to confirm this interpretation. The Paingins must be considered as a Brahmana-charana, for there is a Paingyam, the work of a Paingin, quoted in the Kaushitaki-brahmana, and in a doubtful passage of the Aitareya-brahmana. It is difficult to say anything equally positive about the Kausiki kalpah, an"? expression which may possibly refer to the Kausika-sutras of the Atharva-veda.

Sutras. Unless, therefore, a quotation can be brought forward previous to Katyayana, and referring to a collection of Kalpa-sutras, such quotation calling the Sutras not by the name of their author, but by the name of a Charana, not in the singular, but in the masculine plural, Kumarila's distinction between Brahmanas and Sutras remains unshaken, and we are justified in maintaining that the Kalpa-sutras, in spite of some apparent similarity with the later Brahmanas, belong to a period of literature different in form and character from that which preceded it, and which gave rise to the traditionary literature of the Brahmanas.

The Kalpa-sutras are important in the history of Vedic literature for more than one reason. They not only mark a new period of literature, and a new purpose in the literary and religious life of India, but they contributed to the gradual extinction of the numerous Brahmanas, which to us are therefore only known by name. The introduction of a Kalpa-sutra was the introduction of a new book of liturgy. If it was adopted by different Charanas, smaller differences in the ceremonial and its allegorical interpretation, which had been kept up by the Brahmanas of each Charana, would gradually be merged in one common ceremonial; or, if they were considered of sufficient importance, a short mention, such as we find here and there in the Sutras, would suffice, and render the tedious discussions of the Brahmanas on the same points, superfluous. If the Sutras were once acknowledged as authoritative, they became the most important part of the sacred literature which a Brahman had to study. Those who had to perform the sacrifices might do so without the Veda, simply by means KALPA. 187 of the Kalpa-sutras ; but no one could learn the ceremonial from the hymns and Brahmanas alone, without the help of the Sutras.1 There remained, indeed, the duty of every Brahman to learn his Svadhyaya, which comprised the hymns and the Brahmanas. But complaints were made, at least at a later time, that the hymns and the Brahmanas were neglected on account of the Sutras, and one of the reasons why the Hotri priests were never allowed to have a prayer-book, such as the hymn-books of the Yajur-veda and Sama-veda, was the fear that they would then neglect their Svadhyaya, and learn only those hymns which were enjoined for the sacrifices by the Kalpa-sutras. We need not wonder, therefore, if, after a short time, the authors of Kalpasutras became themselves the founders of new Charanas, in which the Sutras were considered the most essential portion of the sacred literature ; so that the hymns and Brahmanas were either neglected, or kept up under the name of " the hymns and Brahmanas of the new Charana," having ceased to form by themselves the foundation of an independent tradition or school.

In order to make quite clear the influence which the Sutras exercised on the final constitution of the Vedic Charanas we ought to distinguish between three classes of Charanas: 1. Those which originated with the texts of Sanhit&s; 2. Those which originated with the texts of Brahmanas ; 3. Those which originated with the Sutras.
Sr^rf^TT ^f^^JW^Uj^MchldLJI Kumarila.

We need not enter here into the question, whether originally there was but one Veda, and whether this original Veda became afterwards divided into three branches or Sakhas, the Rig-veda, Yajur-veda, and Sama-veda. This is the view adopted by the Brahmans, and they consider these three divisions as the three most ancient Sakhas, and their propagators or pravartakas as the three most ancient Charanas. This is a natural mistake. It is the same mistake which leads to the assumption of a common literary language previous to the existence of the spoken dialects, whereas in fact the various dialects existed previous to the establishment of the classical language. The first collection of Vedic hymns is that which we have in the Rig-veda, a collection, not made with any reference to the threefold division of the later ceremonial, and therefore not one of three branches, but the original stock, to which the other two, the Yajur-veda and Sama-veda, were added at a much later period.

The most ancient Sakhas and Charanas of which we have any knowledge are those which arose from differences in the text of the Rig-veda-sanhita, such as the Bashkalas and Sakalas. We never hear of either Brahmanas or Sutras peculiar to these sakhas, and the natural conclusion, confirmed besides by native authority, is that they diverged and became separated on the strength of various readings and other peculiarities, affecting the texts of their Sanhitas. There is no evidence as to the existence of similar Sanhita-sakhas for the Yajur-veda or Samaveda. If we take the two sakhas of the Yajur-vedasanhita, that of the Kanvas and that of the Madhyandinas, both presuppose the existence of a Vaja- KALPA. 189 saneyi-sanhita, and this Vajasaneyi-sanhita would have been perfectly useless without a Brahmana. It was not the Sanhita, but the Brahmana of the Vajasaneyins, handed down as it was in various texts, which gave rise to the fifteen Charanas of the Vajasaneyins, and among them to the Kanva and Madhyandinacharanas. Their Sanhitas were of secondary importance, and startling as such an opinion might sound to an orthodox Brahman, were probably not put together till after the composition of the Vajasaneyi-brahmana in its original and primitive form. The peculiar differences in the text of the Mantras of the Kanvas and Madhyandinas depend on the differences occurring in their respective Brahmanas, and not vice versd. On the same ground we must doubt the existence of ancient Sanhita-sakhas for the Samaveda.

The next step which led to the formation of Charanas was the adoption of a Brahmana, and we therefore call this second class the Brahmana-charanas. When the growth of a more complicated ceremonial led to the establishment of three or four classes of priests, each performing peculiar duties, and requiring a special training for their sacerdotal office, there must have been a floating stock of Brahmanas, or dicta theologica, peculiar to each class of priests. They treated of the general arrangement of the sacrifice. They handed down the authoritative opinions of famous sages : they gave the objections raised against such opinions by other persons : and gradually they clothed these contradictory statements in the form of a logical argument. Occasionally an allegorical interpretation was given of the meaning of certain rites, the simple and natural import of which had been for- 190 KALPA.

gotten. Kewards were vouchsafed to the pious worshipper, and instances were recorded of such rewards having been obtained by the faithful of former ages. All these sayings and discussions were afterwards collected as three distinct Brahmanas, belonging to the three classes of priests. We still meet with the general names of Bahvricha-brahmanas for the Rig-veda, of Adhvaryubrahmanas for the Yajur-veda, and of Chhandoga-brahmanas for the Sama-veda, without any further reference to particular Charanas by which these Brahmanas were collected or adopted. But those Brahmanas are no longer met with in their original form. They have come down to us, without exception, as the Brahmanas of certain Charanas of each Veda. Instead of one Bahvricha-brahmana of the Rigveda, we only find the Bahvricha-brahmana of the Aitareyins, or the Kaushitakins, or the Sankhayanins. Instead of one Chhandoga-brahmana or Chhandogyam, we have the Chhandoga-brahmana of the T&ndins or the Tandya, and we find quotations from other Charanas, such as the Satyayanins1 or the Kauthumas.

1 In one of the most interesting Brahmanas of the Chhandogas, the Samavidhana-brahmana, we see how the two last in a series of teachers became the founders of a Charana, by teaching this Brahmana, which had been handed down to them through a succession of nine or at least six masters, to a multitude of followers.

KALPA. 191 Instead of one Adhvaryu-brahmana, we have the dark code of the old Charakas, or the Taittiriyas and the kathas, and the new Brahmana of the Vajasaneyins, and their descendants, the Kanvas and Madhyandinas. We nowhere find the original collection from which the various recensions might be supposed to have branched off and deviated in time. In most cases, where we possess the texts of a Brahmana, preserved by different iSakhas, the variations are but small, and they point clearly to one and the same original from which they descended. Sometimes, however, the variations are of a different kind, so much so that we are inclined to admit several independent collections of that floating stock of Brahmanic lore, which went on accumulating in different places and through various generations. If we compare the Brahmanas of the Aitareyins and the Kaushitakins, we find their wording, even where they treat of the same matters, very different. The order in which the sacrifices are described is not always the same, nor are the ceremonial rules always identical. Illustrations and legends are interspersed in the Brahmana of the Kaushitakins of which no trace can be found in the Brahmana of the Aitareyins. And yet, with all these differences, the literal coincidence of whole chapters, the frequent occurrence of the same sentences, the same comparisons and illustrations, render it impossible to ascribe to each of these Brahmanas a perfectly independent origin. The two Brahmanas of the Kanvas and Madhyandinas, in spite of their differences, in spite of f^Wfl rrff%arr^l*jfa«iY ^jPSf: II On the &atyayanins and their relation to the Saraa-veda, see Indische Studien, i. 49.

additions and omissions that have been pointed out in either, compel us to admit that they had a common starting-point. To judge from frequent quotations, the number of Brahmanas, differing from each other more or less considerably, and the number of Charanas, founded on these Brahmanas, must have been very large. We can easily imagine how this happened. The name of a famous teacher, who gathered a number of students around himself in a village, or who lived under the protection of some small Raja, was preserved by his pupils for generations. The sacred literature which he was, perhaps, the first to teach in a newly-founded colony, was afterwards handed down under the sanction of his name, though differing but slightly from the traditional texts kept up in the community from which he himself had started. He might, perhaps, add a few chapters of his own composition, a change quite sufficient, in the eyes of the Brahmans to constitute a new work, or at least to disqualify it for claiming any longer its original title. When these new Charanas had once been founded, it was but natural, though they originated chiefly with a Brahmana of their own, that the text of their Sanhitas also should be slightly modified. This was not the case necessarily. The Aitareyins, for instance, and the Kaushitakins, though they differed in their Brahmanas, preserved, as far as we know, the same sakha of the Sanhita, and preserved it each with the same minute accuracy. No Sanhita peculiar to the Kaushitakins and Aitareyins is ever mentioned, and the points on which they differed were, from the very first, connected with the subject matter of the Brahmanas. Students following different sakhas, as far as their Brahmana was concerned, might very well KALPA. 193 follow one and the same Sakha of the Sanhita, though they would no longer call it by its own original name. In most cases, however, and particularly in the Charanas of the Yajur-veda, a difference in the Brahmanas would necessitate, or, at least, naturally lead to, corresponding differences in the Sanhita, such as we find, for instance, in the hymns of the Kanvas and Madhyandinas.1 These Brahmana-charanas existed previous to the first composition of the Sutras, and in the Sutras belonging to the Sama-veda, which are the earliest Sutras we possess, they are quoted. No Sutra is ever quoted in any of the Brahmanas, but there is no collection of Sutras in which the various Sakhas of the Brahmanas are not referred to by name. The authorities quoted in the Sutras on doubtful points of the Vedic ceremonial, are invariably taken from the Brahmana-charanas. In the commentary on Panini, such names as " the Aitareyins, the Satyayanins, and Bhallavins " are distinctly explained as supporters of ancient Brahmanas ; and the antiquity of the two last is still further confirmed by the fact of their being quoted as Brahmanic authorities in the Satapatha brahmana.2 The third and most modern class of Charanas consists of those which derive their origin from the introduction of a new body of Sutras, such as the Asvalayaniyas, the Katyayaniyas, and many of the subdivisions of the Taittiriyas. It is not always possible to determine with certainty whether a Charana dates 1 The differences of these schools may be seen in Weber's edition of the Yajur-veda at the end of each Adhyaya.
2 See Weber's Indische Studien, ii. 44.

from the Brahmana period, or from the Sutra period, because so many of the Brahmanas and Sutras have been lost, and some of the Brahmanas have been handed down to us under the names of more modern Sutra-charanas, by which they were adopted. It is easy to determine that the Kaushitakins date from the Brahmana period, because there is neither a Kaushitaki-sutra nor a Kaushitaki-sanhita, but only a Kaushitaki-brahmana ; but in other instances our knowledge of the ancient literature of India is too fragmentary to enable us to ^x the age of the numerous Charanas which are quoted by later authorities. Some of the Sutras again, as we saw before, are older than others, and seem almost to trespass on the frontiers of the Brahmana period. How are we to determine, for instance, whether the Sankhayanas were originally a Brahman a-charana, and had their Sutras written by one of their own sect, or whether the foundation of their Charana rested on the text of the Sutras1, a new text of the original Brahmana of the Bahvrichas being adopted by them in later times, and thenceforth quoted as the JSankhayana-brahmana ? In some instances the relative age of certain Sutras has been preserved by the tradition of the schools. Thus the most ancient Sutra of the Taittiriyas is said to have been that of Baudhayana, who was succeeded by Bhara dvaja, Apastamba, Satyashadha Hiranyakesin, Vadhuna, and Vaikhanasa ; all of whom, with the exception of the two last, have lent their names to different Charanas of the dark Yajur-veda.
1 It should be observed, that in some MSS. of the Charana vyuha the two Charanas, which belong most likely to the Sautra period, those of Asvalayana and &ankhayana, are not mentioned.

KALPA. 195 Although none of the Sutras seem to have been written with the distinct purpose of founding a new Charana, it can easily be imagined how different communities, after adopting a collection of Sutras as the highest authority for their ceremonial, became inclined to waive minor points of difference in the Sanhitas and Brahmanas, and thus coalesced into a new Charana under the name and sanction of their Sutrakara. After these new Sautra-charanas had once been started, we find that the Sanhitas and Brahmanas, current among their members, were designated by the name of the new Charanas. Thus we may explain the title of Asvalayana-brahmana given to the Aitareyabrahmana in one of the MSS. of the Bodleian library1; and we shall not hesitate to ascribe the same meaning to an Asvalayana-brahmana, said to be quoted by Yajnikadeva in his commentary on Katyayana.2 Why such a Brahmana should not be quoted by early writers, such as the authors of Sutras, is easily understood. Its title was necessarily of late origin, and it is important as marking the progressive changes in the nomenclature of Indian literature. We have a similar and still better authenticated instance in the so-called Apastamba-brahmana, which is but a different title of the Taittiriya-brahmana, as adopted by the followers of the Apastamba-sutras. It is in this manner that the Sutras may be said to have contributed partly to the formation of new Charanas, some of which are not mentioned in the ancient lists, as, for instance, the K&tyayaniyas ; partly to the extinction of 1 MS. Wilson, 473. The title is ^TOCTlFrar sflipr (sic) J it contains the fifth Book of the Aitareya-brahmana.
2 Katyayana, ii. 5. 18.; vi. 6. 5. Indische Studien, i. 230.
o 2 196 KALPA.
the more ancient Brahmana-charanas and Sanhita charanas, many of which are now known to us by name only.

That the introduction of the Sutras and the foundation of Sutracharanas was felt as an innovation by the Brahmans themselves, we perceive from the manner in which even modern writers speak of them ; half objecting to their authority, yet glad to admit and even to defend what could no longer be prevented. The Sutras were not, indeed, admitted as part of the Sruti, yet they were made part of the Svadhyaya, and had to be learned by heart by the young student. They might, therefore, like the Sanhitas and Brahmanas, claim a kind of sacred character, and in time become the charter of a new Charana. Thus we read in Mahadeva's Commentary on the Hiranyakesi-sutras l : ." The Kalpa-sutra is sometimes different for different Sakhas, sometimes it is not. The difference of the Sakhas arises partly from a difference of the sacred texts (adhyayana being used in the sense of svddhyaya, perhaps with reference to the peculiar pronunciation taught in the Pratisakhyas), partly from a difference in the Sutras. The Sutras of Asvalayana and Katyayana, for instance, are the same for KALPA. 197 two Sakhas whose respective texts are different, while in the Taittiriya-veda we find Sakhas with different Sutras, but no differences in their sacred texts. Hence it may be said l, that sometimes, where there is a difference in the Sutras, there is also a difference of 6akha ; and, on the other hand, where there is a difference of Sakha, there may be a difference in the Sutras." Mahadeva goes even further, and tries to show that, like all the revealed literature of the Brahmans, the Sutras also existed previous to the beginning of time and had no historical origin.2 "As the various Sakhas," he says, " which arise from various readings of the sacred texts are without a beginning, or eternal, so are also the various oakhas which arise from different Sutras. For the titles of certain Sutras, derived from their authors, are not modern ; but being eternal, as inherent in individual Rishis, whose names occur in certain Kalpas or ceremonials, and retaining the same character when applied to the Sutras, which have been promulgated by the Rishis, they hold good as titles for 1 Afterwards he says again : ff^Tr ^3^1 ^rfrTCfalU <o|| «( i ^"fSTrf t II "It has been shown in the Charanavyuha, that in the Taittiriya-sakha, where there is but one and the same sacred text, subordinate Sakhas arise from different Sutras."
" Nanakalpa-gatasu " cannot refer to the chronological Kalpas, because these are after the beginning of time.
o 3 198 KALPA.

Sakhas, which apparently are marked by the names of men." l We may now understand in what sense the same Mahadeva gives to the word Charana the meaning both of Sakha and Sutra. " It is true," he says, "that sakha means a part of the sacred tradition, consisting of Mantras and Brahmanas, and that the subordinate sakhas of the Yeda owe their origin to the differences of either Mantras or Brahmanas. Nevertheless, as Veda means the sacred tradition, together with the Angas or subsidiary doctrines, a sakha may include the Angas and yet remain Yeda, and as such become different from other sakhas, owing to a difference in the Angas. If, therefore, the Sutra, which is an Anga, differs, there will be difference in the sacred tradition; and thus a difference in the Sutras may well become the cause of a different name of a Sakha." 2 The following list, though far from being complete, contains some of the Kalpa-sutras which are best known to us either from MSS. or otherwise : —

I. Yajur-veda. A. Old, or Dark Text 1. Apastamba, text and commentary existing.
zh: KALPA。183 部被保存下來。但是，儘管沃爾夫的格言主張散文文獻處處標誌著書寫的引入，我們必須如同對待讚歌一樣，也為《梵書》主張存在著某個時期，在此期間它們純粹透過口頭傳統得以保存。若不承認口頭傳統的存在——這種傳統歷經數代、由不同家族與婆羅門殖民地在多個地點進行傳承——似乎無法解釋同一《梵書》為何存在眾多的版本，以及每個版本內部為何存在各種異文。原始《梵書》合集的變動、增補與重新編排如何進行，我們無法確定；但我們可以看出，《沙達帕塔梵書》的康瓦與馬迪亞迪那兩個版本都預設了某個共同的起點。同樣的情況適用於《百讚梵書》中艾達瑞耶派、桑克亞亞派或考什塔基派之間相差懸殊的版本。每部吠陀都存在著《梵書》中的共同基礎。相同的儀式被描述，相同的疑問被提出，類似的解答被提議，許多章節以相同的措辭被重複。在每個版本成形之前——而我們保存下來的眾多版本中只有少數——它們必定曾經流傳於人手之間，有時喪失舊的內容，有時蒐集新的內容；時而支離破碎，時而重新安排，直到最後其作者的名字被融合於保存其著作的詵羅那（Charana）的名字之中。在《經文》中看不到這類痕跡。我們現在在手稿中讀到的內容，很可能正是最初由迦底耶衍那、阿濕婆利衍那及其他人所撰寫的樣貌。它們顯然是個人著作，
雙重作者制的成果，乃是謹慎而系統性研究的產物。它們預設了《桑希塔》（Sanhitas）及《桑希塔》的《支派》（Sakhas），也預設了《梵書》（Brahmanas）及《梵書》的《支派》（Sakhas）。它們同時亦指涉個別作者，無論這些作者是否成為了《訶羅那派》（Charanas）的創始人，或是否享有作為法律教師及其他與早期婆羅門知識追求相關主題教師的權威。

然而有一個事實似乎與梵書（Brahmanas）和經文（Sutras）的區別相悖，因為它將一個極早的起源及傳承特徵賦予了至少某些以經文形式撰寫的著作。在迦坦耶那（Katyayana）之時，若非在帕尼尼（Panini）之時，已存在一些經文，它們當時並未被視為現代或至少聲名昭著的作者（如阿斯瓦拉耶那〔Asvalayana〕或迦坦耶那）的著作，反而其名稱本身就表明，它們曾經在某個時期是查蘭（Charana）或某所學派的傳統文獻遺產的一部分。它們的書名是按照上古梵書的同樣原則命名的。後綴 in（nini）被加於其公認作者的名字之上，而正如我們所知，這是其作者被視為聖仙（Rishis）或啟示作家的標誌。¹ 它們的著作並非如同所有現代經文那般以單數形式被引用（例如，「這是阿斯瑪拉他的儀軌」，iti kalpa asmarathah），反而——這是印度古代傳統文獻的一個特徵——以陽性複數形式被引用，其文獻著作被設想為 --- ¹ 參見 Pan. iv. 3. 103–110。根據加爾各答版本，106 以後的經文在《大注疏》（Mahabhashya）中未有解說。

其唯一的實質存在乃在於那些閱讀或傳授它們之人的心靈或記憶中。例如，我們發現「帕拉拉里人（Paralarins）、賽拉林人（Sailalins）、卡爾曼迪人（Karmandins）、克里薩斯溫人（Krisasvins）如是說」，然而即使是帕尼尼（Panini）本人的著作亦被引用為「帕尼尼亞（the Paniniyam）」，猶如「帕尼紐（Panineum）」，而非「帕尼涅因人（the Panineyins）」。* 但儘管這些引文涉及經句（Sutras），應當注意到其中沒有一處涉及卡爾帕（Kalpa），即儀式經句。當帕尼尼（Panini，第四卷第三章第105節），或毋寧說是其注疏者，以類似（雖不完全相同）的方式引用卡爾帕著作時，我們必須記住，「平吉卡爾帕（Paingi kalpah）」、「古聖人平吉所教的儀式」、「考西基卡爾帕（Kausiki kalpah）」、「古聖人庫西卡所教的儀式」之類的表述可能涉及梵書（Brahmanas）的部分，這些部分被稱為卡爾帕（kalpa），即儀式，以區別於梵書（brahmana）或純粹的神學討論²；並且無處說明這些古老的卡爾帕是用書寫形式傳承的。 --- ¹ 卡爾帕經句（Kalpa-sutras）是與帕尼尼同時代或甚至在其之後才作成的，例如我們仍然擁有的阿史婆拉亞那（Asvalayana）和迦帝亞那（Katyayana）的經句，以及已失傳的阿斯瑪拉特亞（Asmarathya）的經句。後者在帕尼尼注疏（第四卷第三章第105節）中被引用為卡爾帕的現代著作；然而阿史婆拉亞那在其經句第五卷第13節中把阿斯瑪拉特亞作為權威引用，他追隨此人的意見而拒斥其他教師的看法。參見《阿史婆拉亞那經句》第五卷第13節；《印度學研究》第一卷第45頁。

2 《經文》(Sutra)中「往昔所述婆羅門儀軌中」(Puranaprokteshu brahmanakalpeshu)的措辭似乎證實了這一解釋。派因吉斯(Paingins)必須被視為婆羅門‧卡拉那(Brahmana-charana)，因為有一部派因吉亞(Paingyam)——派因吉(Paingin)之作——在《考希塔基‧婆羅門書》(Kaushitaki-brahmana)及《阿迦尼亞‧婆羅門書》(Aitareya-brahmana)的一個存疑段落中被引用。關於考希基儀軌(Kausiki kalpah)很難得出同樣確定的結論，這個表述可能涉及《阿闥婆吠陀》(Atharva-veda)的考希卡‧經文(Kausika-sutras)。

經文。因此，除非能提出一份早於迦多耶那（Katyayana）的引文，並且涉及一部《羯磨經》（Kalpa-sutras）的文集，該引文不以其作者之名稱呼經文，而以一個誦承派（Charana）之名稱呼——非單數形式，而是陽性複數形式——庫瑪利拉（Kumarila）對《婆羅門書》與《經文》所作的區分才能被推翻。否則，我們有理由主張，《羯磨經》儘管在外表上與後來的《婆羅門書》有某些相似之處，卻屬於一個在形式與特徵上均不同於其前代的文學時期，而那個前代時期正是產生了《婆羅門書》傳統文獻的時期。

《時間法則經》（Kalpa-sutras）在吠陀文獻的歷史中之所以重要，理由不止一端。它們不僅標誌著文獻的新時期，以及印度宗教與文學生活的新目的，而且促成了眾多《梵書》（Brahmanas）的逐漸式微，以致於我們今日對之多所聞知而已。《時間法則經》的引入乃是新的禮儀書的引入。若《時間法則經》被不同的《誦詩派》（Charanas）所採納，則各《誦詩派》之《梵書》中所保持的儀式細節及其寓言詮釋之較小差異，將逐漸匯合為一種共同的儀式；或者，倘若這些差異被認為具有足夠的重要性，《經文》（Sutras）中所見散見的簡短提及就已足夠，並使得《梵書》對同一議題的冗長論辯變得多餘。一旦《經文》被公認為具權威性，它們就成為婆羅門必須研習的聖典中最重要的部分。那些必須執行祭祀的人可以不藉助吠陀而執行，僅憑《時間法則經》就足夠了；但沒有人能夠單靠讚歌與《梵書》而不借助《經文》之力來學習儀式。確實，每位婆羅門仍有責任學習其《自我研習》（Svadhyaya），此中涵括讚歌與《梵書》。然而至少在後世，人們抱怨讚歌與《梵書》因為《經文》而遭受忽視，而何以《祭祀火祭司》（Hotri）祭司從未被允許擁有《夜柔吠陀》（Yajur-veda）與《娑摩吠陀》（Sama-veda）那樣的讚歌書，其中一項理由便是恐怕他們會因此忽視其《自我研習》，而僅學習那些由《時間法則經》為祭祀所規定的讚歌。是故，我們毋庸詫異，何以在短期之後，《時間法則經》的作者本身即成為新《誦詩派》的創立者，在這些新《誦詩派》中，《經文》被視為聖典中最本質的部分；以至於讚歌與《梵書》要麼遭受忽視，要麼在「新《誦詩派》的讚歌與《梵書》」之名下被保留下來，已不復成為獨立傳統或學派的基礎。
為了清楚說明《經文》（Sutras）對吠陀派系（Vedic Charanas）最終構成所產生的影響，我們應當區分三類派系：1. 源於《本集》（Sanhits）文本者；2. 源於《梵書》（Brahmanas）文本者；3. 源於《經文》者。
我很遺憾，您提供的英文原文似乎出現了編碼錯誤或掃描損毀。所顯示的內容為： Sr^rf^TT ^f^^JW^Uj^MchldLJI Kumarila. 這不是可讀的英文文本，而是亂碼或損毀的字符。其中只有人名 "Kumarila"（庫瑪利拉）能夠辨認。 請您： 1. 確認英文原文是否正確複製 2. 提供清晰、完整的英文原文文本 3. 重新提交翻譯請求 我將樂意為您翻譯正確的穆勒原文。

我們在此無須涉入一個問題，即原本是否只有一部吠陀，以及這部原始的吠陀是否後來被分成三個分支或「沙卡」（Sakha），亦即梨俱吠陀、夜柔吠陀和娑摩吠陀。這是婆羅門所採納的觀點，他們將這三個分部視為三個最古老的沙卡，而其傳播者或「帕瓦他迦」（pravartakas）則視為三個最古老的「查拉納」（Charanas）。這是一個自然而然的錯誤。這與導致人們假設在口語方言存在之前曾有一種共通的文學語言的錯誤如出一轍，然而事實上，各種方言的存在早於古典語言的確立。現存的第一個吠陀讚歌集合就是梨俱吠陀，這個集合並非以任何後來儀式的三分法為參考而編纂，因此並非三個分支之一，而是原始的主幹，其他兩部，即夜柔吠陀和娑摩吠陀，是在相當晚得多的時期才被添加上去的。

我們所知最古老的商羯羅派（Sakhas）和誦經派（Charanas）乃是源於《梨俱吠陀‧本集》（Rig-veda-sanhita）經文差異而產生者，如巴什卡羅派（Bashkalas）和薩卡羅派（Sakalas）。我們從未聽聞這些商羯羅派有任何特有的《梵書》（Brahmanas）或《經書》（Sutras），而根據本地權威的証實，自然的結論是：它們乃因各種異文及其他影響其本集（Sanhitas）文本的特殊性而分化並分離開來。至於《夜柔吠陀》（Yajur-veda）或《娑摩吠陀》（Samaveda），我們找不到類似的本集商羯羅派（Sanhita-sakhas）的證據。若取《夜柔吠陀‧本集》的兩個商羯羅派，即康瓦派（Kanvas）和摩陀延地那派（Madhyandinas），兩者皆預設了一部《瓦亞薩尼依‧本集》（Vajasaneyi-sanhita）的存在。此《瓦亞薩尼依‧本集》若無《梵書》相伴，將毫無用處。正是《瓦亞薩尼依派的梵書》而非本集，以其多種文本形式流傳下來，才產生了瓦亞薩尼依派的十五個誦經派，其中包括康瓦和摩陀延地那誦經派。它們各自的本集乃是次要的，這種看法雖然對正統的婆羅門而言似乎駭人聽聞，但這些本集很可能是在《瓦亞薩尼依梵書》原始本原形式之後才編纂完成的。康瓦派和摩陀延地那派的《咒文》（Mantras）文本中的特殊差異取決於其各自《梵書》中出現的差異，而非相反。基於同樣的理由，我們必須懷疑《娑摩吠陀》是否存在古老的本集商羯羅派。

導向查蘭那（Charanas）形成的下一個步驟，是採納了婆羅門文獻，因此我們把第二類稱為婆羅門查蘭那（Brahmana-charanas）。當日益複雜的儀式發展導致了三個或四個祭司等級的建立，每個等級各自執行特殊的職務，並為其神聖職位而需要特殊的訓練時，必然存在一種流動的婆羅門文獻或神學公式（dicta theologica）的儲備，各自屬於每一個祭司等級。它們處理了祭祀的一般安排。它們傳承了著名聖賢的權威性意見：它們記載了其他人針對這類意見所提出的反對意見：而且逐漸地，它們把這些相互矛盾的陳述納入邏輯論證的形式。偶爾，對某些儀式的含義給出了寓言式的解釋，而這些儀式簡單而自然的意義本來曾經…

已獲得。虔誠的祭祀者獲得了獎賞，並有記錄表明前代信眾確實獲得過此類獎賞。所有這些言論與討論後來被收集成三部各自獨立的《梵書》，分別屬於三類祭司。我們仍然遇到《梵書》的通稱名——《巴夫里遮梵書》（Bahvricha-brahmanas）用於梨俱吠陀、《阿特瓦里尤梵書》（Adhvaryu-brahmanas）用於夜柔吠陀、《讚歌梵書》（Chhandoga-brahmanas）用於娑摩吠陀——但這些通稱並未進一步提及收集或採納這些《梵書》的特定傳承系統。然而，那些《梵書》已不再以原始形式留存。它們流傳至今，無一例外，都是各吠陀特定傳承系統的《梵書》。《梨俱吠陀》的《巴夫里遮梵書》不再是單數，我們只能找到艾塔瑞亞派（Aitareyins）或考希塔基派（Kaushitakins）或商卡亞尼派（Sankhayanins）的《巴夫里遮梵書》。《讚歌梵書》或《讚歌奧義書》（Chhandogyam）不再是單數，我們擁有坦丁派（Tândins）或坦亞派（Tandya）的《讚歌梵書》，並且我們發現來自其他傳承系統的引文，例如薩蒂亞亞尼派（Satyayanins）或考圖瑪派（Kauthumas）。
在最有趣的一些婆羅門經典中，即頌讚奧義書（Chhandogas）的《相同儀禮婆羅門經》（Samavidhana-brahmana）中，我們看到一系列教師中最後的兩位，透過向眾多追隨者傳授這部已經由至少六位或九位大師代代相傳下來的婆羅門經典，成為某一傳承學派（Charana）的創始人。

**KALPA。191** 我們沒有單一的Adhvaryu-brahmana，反而擁有古代Charakas的晦澀法典，或Taittiriyas和Kathas的法典，以及Vajasaneyins的新Brahmana，以及他們的後裔Kanvas和Madhyandinas。我們在任何地方都找不到原始集成，據此可以推測各種版本在時間流逝中可能已經分化和偏離。在大多數情況下，當我們擁有由不同的Sakhas所保存的Brahmana文本時，其差異甚微，它們清楚地指向同一個原始來源，各版本皆由之衍生。然而，有時差異的性質截然不同，以至於我們傾向於承認存在多個獨立的Brahmanic知識浮動資料庫的集成，這些資料庫在不同的地方和不同的世代中不斷累積。倘若我們比較Aitareyins和Kaushitakins的Brahmanas，我們會發現它們的措辭，即便在涉及相同主題之處，也差別很大。祭祀被描述的順序並非總是相同，儀式規則也並非總是相同。在Kaushitakins的Brahmana中散布著圖示和傳說，而在Aitareyins的Brahmana中完全找不到這些內容的痕跡。然而，儘管有所有這些差異，整章的字面巧合、相同句子的頻繁出現、相同的比較和圖示，使得我們不可能將這兩個Brahmanas各自歸為完全獨立的起源。Kanvas和Madhyandinas的兩個Brahmanas，儘管各有差異，儘管……

人們已經指出的增刪之處，迫使我們承認它們必有共同的起點。從頻繁的引述來判斷，梵書（Brahmanas）的數量必定極其龐大，它們彼此之間的差異或多或少都相當可觀，而基於這些梵書所建立的傳承派系（Charanas）的數量也同樣眾多。我們不難想像這是如何發生的。一位著名教師的名字會被他的弟子代代相傳，無論他是在某個村落聚集了眾多學生，抑或是在某位小王公的庇護下生活。他也許是首次在新開拓的殖民地傳授的聖典文獻，後來便以他的名義代代相傳，儘管其內容與他本人所出發的社群中保存的傳統文本略有差異。他或許會加入自己創作的若干章節，這種改動在婆羅門眼中足以構成一部新的著作，或至少足以使其不再能聲稱具有原來的頭銜。一旦這些新的傳承派系確立之後，雖然它們主要源自其本身的一位梵書家，但其《梵經》（Sanhitas）的文本也自然而然地會略有修改，這並非必然的情況。例如艾達瑞耶派（Aitareyins）和考希塔金派（Kaushitakins）儘管在梵書上有所不同，但據我們所知，他們在《梵經》的傳承分支（sakha）上卻保存了相同的版本，而且各自以同樣精確細緻的方式保存。從未曾提及過任何專屬於考希塔金派和艾達瑞耶派的《梵經》，他們所不同之處從一開始就與梵書的主題內容相關聯。不同傳承分支的學生，就其梵書而言，完全可以遵循同一個《梵經》傳承分支，儘管他們不再以其原本的名稱來稱呼它。然而在多數情況下，特別是在夜柔吠陀（Yajur-veda）的傳承派系中，梵書上的差異會導致或至少自然引發《梵經》上相應的差異，例如我們在坎瓦派（Kanvas）和中日派（Madhyandinas）的頌歌中所發現的情況。¹ 這些梵書傳承派系（Brahmana-charanas）早於蘇特拉經（Sutras）的首次成文而存在，在屬於娑摩吠陀（Sama-veda）的蘇特拉經中——這是我們現存最古老的蘇特拉經——它們被引述。在任何梵書中從未曾引述蘇特拉經，但不存在任何蘇特拉經集成不以名稱指涉梵書的各種傳承分支。在蘇特拉經中引述的、用以判決吠陀祭儀疑難點的權威人物，無一例外地都取自梵書傳承派系。在帕尼尼（Panini）的註疏中，「艾達瑞耶派、薩提亞耶尼派和跋羅婆派」之類的名稱被清楚地解釋為古代梵書的支持者；而最後兩派的古老性更因為它們在《百路梵書》（Satapatha brahmana）² 中被引述為梵書權威而得到進一步的確認。傳承派系的第三類，也是最晚出現的一類，由那些源自新蘇特拉經文獻體系的派系所組成，例如阿斯瓦拉亞尼派（Asvalayaniyas）、卡提亞亞尼派（Katyayaniyas）和泰帝利耶派（Taittiriyas）的許多分支。要確定某個傳承派系的年代並不總是可能的， ¹ 這些學派的差異可見於韋伯（Weber）的夜柔吠陀版本中每章的末尾。 ² 《百路梵書》。
見韋伯《印度研究》第二卷第44頁。

來自婆羅門時期或蘇特拉時期，因為許多婆羅門書和蘇特拉已經遺失，某些婆羅門書是以較晚期蘇特拉派系（sutra-charanas）的名義傳承給我們的，這些派系採納了它們。很容易判定考什塔金人（Kaushitakins）出自婆羅門時期，因為既沒有考什塔基蘇特拉（Kaushitaki-sutra）也沒有考什塔基聖典（Kaushitaki-sanhita），只有考什塔基婆羅門書（Kaushitaki-brahmana）；但在其他情況下，我們對古代印度文獻的認識過於零碎，無法確定後世權威所引用的眾多派系（Charanas）的年代。有些蘇特拉如我們之前所見比其他蘇特拉更古老，且幾乎似乎觸及了婆羅門時期的邊界。例如，我們如何確定商卡亞那派（Sankhayanas）最初是否為婆羅門派系（Brahmana-charana），其蘇特拉是由其自身派系的成員撰寫，或者他們派系的基礎是否建立在蘇特拉的文本之上，後來採納了眾誦吠陀（Bahvrichas）原始婆羅門書的新文本，並因此引用為商卡亞那婆羅門書（Sankhayana-brahmana）？在某些情況下，特定蘇特拉的相對年代已被各派系的傳統保留下來。因此據說泰提利亞派（Taittiriyas）最古老的蘇特拉是博陀亞那（Baudhayana）的著作，他之後依次是婆羅陀闍（Bharadvaja）、阿帕斯坦巴（Apastamba）、薩耶沙達（Satyashadha）、希蘭亞克什（Hiranyakesin）、瓦度那（Vadhuna）和瓦卡納沙（Vaikhanasa）；除了後兩位外，他們都用自己的名字給黑色雅柔吠陀（dark Yajur-veda）的不同派系命名。
應該注意的是，在《誦法布局》（Charana vyuha）的某些手抄本中，最有可能屬於素特羅時期（Sautra period）的兩個誦法——阿斯瓦拉亞那（Asvalayana）和商卡亞那（Sankhayana）——並未被提及。

雖然蘇特羅經（Sutras）似乎沒有任何一部是為著明確目的而撰寫以創立新的遮羅那（Charana）的，但可以想見，不同的社群在採納某部蘇特羅經集合作為其祭儀最高權威之後，勢必傾向於放寬《森希塔》（Sanhitas）和《梵書》（Brahmanas）中的細微差異，從而在蘇特羅經作者（Sutrakara）的名義和認可下，聚合為一個新的遮羅那。這些新的蘇特羅遮羅那（Sautra-charanas）一經創立，我們就發現其成員間流傳的《森希塔》和《梵書》，開始以新遮羅那的名稱來稱呼。因此，我們可以解釋為何在牛津大學博德萊圖書館某份手稿中，《阿伊塔瑞亞梵書》被冠以「阿斯瓦拉亞那梵書」之名1；我們也不應遲疑將同樣的含義歸於雅日尼卡德瓦（Yajnikadeva）在其關於迦帝亞那（Katyayana）的評論中所引用的所謂「阿斯瓦拉亞那梵書」2。為何這樣的梵書在早期作者（諸如蘇特羅經的作者）的著作中不見被引用，其理由是顯而易見的。其名稱必然是後出的，而這在標示印度文獻命名法的漸進性變化方面至為重要。我們在所謂的「阿帕斯坦巴梵書」（Apastamba-brahmana）中有一個類似而更有確鑿證據的例子，它不過是被阿帕斯坦巴蘇特羅經（Apastamba-sutras）的追隨者所採納的《泰提里亞梵書》（Taittiriya-brahmana）的另一種名稱。蘇特羅經正是以這種方式部分地促進了新遮羅那的形成，其中有些在古代的名單中並未提及，例如迦帝亞那派（Katyayaniyas）；部分地導致了 1 手稿，威爾遜本，473。標題為 ^TOCTlFrar sflipr（如此）；其包含《阿伊塔瑞亞梵書》的第五卷。 2
2 Katyayana, ii. 5. 18.; vi. 6. 5. *Indische Studien*, i. 230.
2 196 劫（Kalpa）
更為古老的《梵書》（Brāhmaṇa）傳承與《本集》（Saṁhitā）傳承，其中許多現今已不為人知，僅存其名。

蘇特拉（Sutra）的引入及蘇特拉家系（Sutra-charanas）的建立被婆羅門人本身感受為一種創新，我們可以從甚至現代作家談論它們的方式中看出端倪；他們半是反對其權威性，卻又樂於承認、甚至為那些已無法阻止的事物辯護。蘇特拉確實未被承認為吠陀啟示（Sruti）的一部分，然而它們被列入自修誦讀（Svadhyaya）的範疇，年輕學生必須將其背誦成誦。因此，它們可以如同森希塔集（Sanhitas）與婆羅門書（Brahmanas）一樣，主張某種神聖的特性，並逐漸成為新家系（Charana）的憲章。因此我們在摩訶提婆（Mahadeva）的《金胎蘇特拉註疏》（Commentary on the Hiranyakesi-sutras）中讀到：「卡爾帕蘇特拉（Kalpa-sutra）有時因不同的薩卡（Sakha）而異，有時則否。薩卡之異源自聖典之異（adhyayana乃指svadhyaya，或許涉及《音韻書》（Pratisakhyas）所教導的特殊發音方式），也源自蘇特拉之異。例如，阿斯瓦拉亞那（Asvalayana）與卡梯亞那（Katyayana）的蘇特拉對於兩個各自聖典不同的薩卡而言是相同的，然而在泰梯利亞吠陀（Taittiriya-veda）中，我們發現薩卡擁有不同的蘇特拉，但其聖典並無差異。因此可以說，有時蘇特拉有異，薩卡亦有異；反之亦然，薩卡有異時，蘇特拉亦可能有異。」摩訶提婆甚至進一步嘗試證明，如同婆羅門人所有的啟示文獻，蘇特拉也存在於時間開始之前，並無歷史起源。他說：「正如各種薩卡因聖典之各種異讀而生，其無始無終、乃至永恆，同樣地，由不同蘇特拉而生的各種薩卡也是如此。蓋某些蘇特拉的名稱，雖從其作者而得名，卻非現代之物；而是永恆的，內在於各個仙人（Rishi），其名字出現在某些卡爾帕（Kalpas）或祭祀儀式中，且當適用於由仙人所傳述的蘇特拉時保持同樣性質，故足以成為其名稱。」
「無數劫中」（Nanakalpa-gatasu）不能指涉編年學意義上的劫（Kalpa），因為這些劫是在時間開始之後的。
○ 3 198 劫（Kalpa）。

薩卡（Sakhas），顯然是以人名標記的。」¹我們現在可以理解同一位摩訶提婆（Mahadeva）如何賦予「跋蹉」（Charana）這個詞既有薩卡又有蘇特拉的含義。他說：「誠然，薩卡是指聖傳的一部分，由頌文（Mantras）和梵書（Brahmanas）組成，而吠陀的下屬薩卡的起源應歸因於頌文或梵書的差異。然而，既然吠陀是指聖傳，連同其附屬學說（Angas），一個薩卡可以包含這些附屬學說，並仍然保持吠陀的身份，並因附屬學說的差異而與其他薩卡有所不同。因此，如果作為附屬學說的蘇特拉不同，聖傳就會有所不同；這樣，蘇特拉的差異就可能成為薩卡另取新名的原因。」² 以下名單雖然遠非完整，但列舉了我們從抄本或其他方式較為熟知的一些劫波蘇特拉（Kalpa-sutras）：
參照第九十七頁，註一。
